So, I'm going to take you through the entire straight line system from front to back and give you one training that sort of takes you through all the steps in the syntax and I'm going to try to position it in a different way than I've ever done it before. So what happened was is I had stumbled upon this niche and when I tested the idea myself, the results that I got and I got with Danny is on my dream apartment, it was so staggering, so amazing that I decided to shut down Stratton the way it was and reinvent the firm. Just the reason I structure this program with daily motivations is because what I learned from that experience is that the daily interaction, the daily pump up, the daily reinforcement of skills is the difference between success and failure for any salesperson. Seriously, without that daily anchor for someone, it's really, really difficult to go it alone. So I've noticed throughout these, I started it there. And I place a big fit X on either end. And I said to these guys, what? Guys, this is your open where the sale begins. And this is your close where the client says, yeah, let's do it. And you open up the sale. And by the way, guys, back in the beginning when you were selling penny stocks, every once in a while, you get one of those perfect lay down sales where everything you'd say, everything you do, the client was like, yes, yes, yes, yes. Almost like a pre sold person right from the very start all the way up to the time that you ask for the order for the first time. And they say, yeah, let's do it. Almost like an objectionless close. So essentially that in my mind was the perfect straight line sale. So this is the perfect straight line sale. And I want to stop here and explain what I mean. The shortest distance mathematically between any two points is what? A straight line. So if you're trying to close someone, you're not going to purposely try to delay it. You're not going to take a circuitous path to close on. You want to close as quickly and efficiently as possible. If you could design a perfect solution yourself, right? It would be, let's take the fucking long way home. You take the short way, right? So that's what I meant by the perfect straight line sale. It's basically one of those rare sales where the client seems to be almost pre sold from the moment he enters the encounter. So everything you say, everything you do, he's like, oh yeah, that sounds good. Oh yeah. And you say, does that make sense? He goes, oh yeah, it's perfect. And you tell him it was exactly as he goes, oh, great. Yeah. You say, now getting started is very simple. He's like, oh, wow, that sounds good. And you ask him, he goes, great, where do I sign up? You say, oh, right here. And he hit me, and he had my credit card. Here's my credit card. That's the perfect straight line sale. And I said to these guys, you know, back when you were selling penny stocks to average moms and pops, every once in a while, you would get one of these perfect laid down sales where they were pre sold almost before they walked in. But in the real world of sales, that's typically not what happens. You see, really, in the real world, what happens is you want to keep your prospect. Your goal would always be you want to not make things go longer than they have to. You'd love for things to stay on the straight line where you basically are in control the whole time and you say, yes, yes, yes, yes, right. So that's what you would love to happen, right? Of course you would. But in the real world, your client, your prospect, tries to take you off the straight line. They interrupt you. They have questions. They have concerns. They'll go off the Pluto. They interrupt you as you're trying to move them from the open to the close. So what you have in selling are these healthy boundaries above and below the line. These are boundaries that represent, so you just slow this down again. What these boundaries represent is how far the conversation can stray from that perfect situation where you're basically doing all the talking. See, when you're on the straight line, directly on the straight line, that represents you, the salesperson doing all the talking and the client sitting there saying, ah ha, oh great, wow. Yes, oh my God, that's so good. Wow, really great. Oh, awesome. And you ask your salesperson, that's what happens when you're on the line, right? But that's fantasy land, right? Although people in consultative, someone will try to pretend that's all. If you just be a consultative, it's a fucking joke, right? It doesn't really work. See, it's not all, right? So what happens in the real world is they're going to interrupt you. They have questions. They have concerns, right? They go off the Pluto, try to ramble on, right? So these healthy boundaries, what they represent is how far the sale can stray off this perfectly straight line while you're still in control. Meaning it doesn't spiral off into a callous Pluto. Meaning you've gone off to Pluto talking about the price of tea in China. Things that basically are not germane to the sale or down here to your anus, which is not a good place to be for anybody. It's most of us, right? Most of us, right? Point is, is that you want to stay inside these boundaries. Now, when you want, and we stop one more time here. So you really understand what that means. Inside these boundaries. So you're off the straight line. So this spot right here, right here and here. This is where the magic actually happens. This is your first four seconds where you have to, I'm going to just go through this separately. Your first four seconds, you must take control of the conversation by being perceived as an expert. That's number one. I'll go back to the second, right? And then you want to use that control to start gathering intelligence. And gathering intelligence means asking questions following certain rules of prospect and certain rules of influence that are allowing you to uncover what someone's needs are, what their values are, what their beliefs are, what their hierarchies of value is, what their pain points are, and allows you to uncover all of that without breaking your point, without pissing them off. In fact, when you're doing this right, you want to, you're asking these questions here, right? And you're actually building massive rapport, you're building rapport. I'll show you how that later, right? So when you ask questions, you're actually building rapport. So these first three steps, number one, you must take control of the sale. That's your first step. And I'll get back to how in a second, right? Number two, you want to use that control, not to talk, talk, talk, talk, talk. You don't want to be a talking head. People hate that, right? You want to use that control to start asking questions. So you can identify their needs, their beliefs, their values, their pain points, right? So you want to then gather, it's called gathering intelligence. And then you want to ask those questions in a certain way and engage in what's called active listening. So when they respond like, uh-huh, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm. Yup, got it, huh? You engage in that active listening so you now build massive rapport on both the conscious and unconscious level. You get that? So these are the three steps. Now watch what I'm going to do. I'm going to take this part right here and let's make that the whole straight line. This is just straight line. I was going to magnify this one section here of intelligence gathering. This whole thing is intelligence gathering, right? Every time you ask a question, so tell me how long have you been living in the area? You've moved forward on the line. You ask another question, okay? And oh, do your kids go to school? You have any kids you have? Okay, where do they go to school, right? What does your wife do? Every time you ask a question, it's directed. It has a purpose. And that purpose means that you move down the line. You're not asking questions for the sake of asking questions. This is called goal oriented communication, meaning the goal is we want to move someone down the straight line and close them. That's your goal. So when you ask someone a question, what's actually happening is, is that question moves you down the straight line. Or else don't fucking ask it. Unless that question gives you some intelligence that you need that allows you to keep going forward. If you didn't have this intelligence, you couldn't go forward. You couldn't close the deal. Don't ask it. You don't ask questions for the sake of sounding smart or wasting time. You don't do that. You ask questions because they have a purpose. They have a meaning. And they give you some intelligence that allows you then to move forward and close the deal. So in essence, each question you ask during your intelligence having phase allows you to move more forward, forward, forward, down the line. And what happens is every time you ask someone a question, what can the prospects say? What are the possibilities? Well, they can do A. They can answer you perfectly. So let's say you. Well, the third, that this one's very rare when they just won't answer, they'll typically just dodge it. And next thing you know, they start spending 15 minutes talking about going and shooting some innocent fucking ducks in northern England and making buck soup. That's like stuff. And I'm, and then at the end of course the sales doesn't close the deal. I was like, damn it. I thought I was so close. Like, no, you weren't, you've never fucking closed. You lost the sale and you started talking about fucking duck hunting. Here's the deal. Experts don't do that. Experts don't have time, the inclination, they're trained too well. They're not going to waste their time talking about things that are not germane to the sale. So what do you do when someone interrupts you? Well, you can't say, whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, listen buddy, you're going off the Pluto here. You're wasting my fucking time. I'm a straight line expert here and us straight line experts. We know that building rapport is not about talking about duck hunting in the hinterlands of northern England. That's repulsive. It's not really rapport building, right? We know rapport is more about the aha, yup, it's about body language. Looking at someone's eyes, listening actively to their responses after you ask them very smart questions. So you straight line experts know that. But if you were to actually say that to your prospect, like, whoa, stop that stuff. Don't stop talking about fucking duck hunting, all right? You're wasting my time here. I'm here to sell you a currency product. Is that going to work very well? No. You're going to lose that sale, you'll break rapport and you'll be done, right? So how do you get around that? Because remember in this little microcosm, what's our goal here? Let's look at this one intelligence gather, right? Let's look at the phase into itself. Just intelligence gather. The goal is we have to get to the end. We have to finish our intelligence. We can't move to the next step in the syntax until we're done gathering all the intelligence we need. We simply can't go forward on the straight line, right? You can't try to close someone until you know what their needs are, what their values are, what their pain points are. That's how you close effectively, right? So the point is, is that while you can't say to someone, buddy, stop. I don't do this rambling and going off talking about the price of tea in China. I'm an expert. So here's what you do. When you ask someone a question, this happens all the time, you say, so tell me how long you're living in the area for. And they say, oh, living here for about four and a half years. Yeah, because before that, I originally came from the southern part of the country here because I really enjoyed duck hunting and I found out something. And then next thing you know, they're spiraling off the Pluto. So what do you do? If you're when you're in personal, when you're like, uh-huh, oh, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, uh-huh. Wow, uh-huh, mm-hmm. You listen, uh-huh, great. It's called listening actively. Uh-huh, yep, got it. Wow, that's pretty cool. And let them spin out. And then when they're done, when they finally spun out and they finished off that ramble, you're like, oh, wow, it's totally cool. Now, watch where they are. So they've spun out here and you just take them right back to the point in the straight line where you left off and you ask the next question in sequence. That's what you do. And once you master this one tactic, it makes your life so much easier. Because what happens is people are always going to spiral out. They're going to ramble on. And when people do that to me, I actually use it as a rapport building moment because I'm like, oh, wow, ha-ha, oh my god, it's so cool. Really? Oh, great, wow. But what I don't do is I don't jump into the nightmare and say, oh my god, I love duck hunting too. Wait, you love shotguns? I love shotguns. You like space travel? I want to be an astronaut. That's fucking rapport too small for that shit. That's not where rapport is. Report is on an unconscious level and it's based on two things. Number one, that you can. You actually care about them. You're not just there to make a fucking commission. You care about the outcome. You have to get them what they want. And number two, you're just like them, but not in the sense that you like what they like, but that you process the world the same way. You speak at generally the same rate of speech, the same way. The same level of voice. You enter their world where they are. You don't come in like if it sounds like a slow talking Southern, I'm like, hey, Jordan don't put, I'll slow my patterns down. Okay. Enter their world where they are. Report is based on that. And most of all, so that you care that you can't. You're not just there to make a commission, which is what most people think about salespeople. All right. So going back to this first night, I said to these guys, don't you guys get it? Every sales the same. Watch, it's a straight line. And I drew that long thin straight line. And you know, here's the deal guys, you know, I don't know if you ever read the book called thinking bro rich. If you haven't read this book, read it. It's worth reading. It talks about this thing called the collective unconscious and the like all that there is. It's like sort of the ether out there. Now you guys know I'm not a woo woo person, right? I don't believe in the shit about I'm very scientifically grounded. I do believe that there are these windows of clarity that certain people tend to open up. And when they open up in your life, if you really jump through with a vengeance, you'll have access to information that you never had access to before. It's like when you hear about a rock group, like, you know, that that is one year they made the most amazing music that albums like the musics, holy shit, they suck forever. Right. I mean, that's what I took on a window of clarity. Well, for me, this, this what I don't know whether it was the anger, the frustration, the fear of my billion dollar idea, having to be abandoned and go back to something, whatever it was, something in the trigger, this window of clarity to open. And all of a sudden I had access to information and ways of explaining things I never had even thought of before. And all happened this night. All right. And I looked at my guys and said, let me tell you this guys, when you, when you, when you're talking to rich people, when you talk to people who are educated, you're talking to people in a position of power and thawing, you got three seconds. Okay. Actually, it was for you got four seconds. Oh, I'll go back to that way with Harvard University. You got four seconds to establish three crucial things, four seconds. And I'll tell you exactly what they are. And if you don't establish these things in those first four seconds, you're done. You can't close anybody. They looked at me like a lot. I said, in those first four seconds, you must be perceived as being number one sharp as attack, sharp as attack. Number two, I can almost write again. I said, enthusiastic as hell. And number three, an expert in your field, an authority figure, an expert in your field will force the record sharp as attack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in your field. You got four seconds to accomplish those three things or else you're done. You can't close anybody. See what I realized and here and I kind of gave us to you in the beginning very quickly. I kind of laid the foundation to this. I said to you when I said, guys, what is so odd about it? I don't get it. I'm doing it. Why can't you do it? And they said, there's too many objections. There's thousands. Oh, yes, thousands. Oh my God, so many. They keep interrupting us. We can't even get our pitches off. And I'm like, wait a second. They can't get their pitches off. It's shocking because usually when I'm selling, I'm getting the same objections they're getting, but I get them at the end after I asked for the order for the first time. Who's going to object to me before I even ask for the order? Why would you say, let me think about it or let me call you back to bad time or got to talk to my local broker before I told you to buy me? It doesn't make sense. Yet my brokers were being abused and batted around by the rich people to the point that they couldn't even get a legitimate pitch off. And that was when it hit me. And I realized as to what it was, is there was a certain way that I had of talking, a certain way I had of moving my body. Something I was doing was was in person, my body language and my tone out a certain way that when people would lay eyes on me, I hear my voice over the phone. Instantly they knew there was something different about me. I was not the average bear. And same thing for Danny. Here's the interesting with Danny. When I trained Danny, he was the first person I trained had a cell stocks. And he actually struggled for a couple of days. And then once he got it, he took off like a rocket. He was unbelievable. But he struggled for it. But once he got it, it was just like second nature to him. Second nature. There was some way that we had, I taught Danny of tonality, confidence. And here's the thing. He was a very educated guy from a wealthy family. His dad was a very famous doctor, the kidney king of a Brookville hospital in New York, Brookville, Brookville hospital. So Danny came from a very educated, very successful family. He was used to being around rich people. His whole life, he wasn't intimidated. He knew he kind of intuitively knew to walk the walk, talk the talk. Yet my 12 schmendricks, right, they'd get in the phone with these rich people and they were completely intimidated, blown away. And they were not coming across being sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in there. So they weren't getting that across in those first four seconds. And because of that, what was happening is rather than the prospect deferring to you and saying, all right, you're an expert, you're sharp. I will essentially defer and you control of the sale because that's what we do in the position in the presence of as we defer and control to it. And control to an expert. What they were doing is they were trying to grab control. Like this guy's a fucking idiot. I'm not going to let him control. They will back my people around because they were not being perceived the right way. See, here's the deal. The reason these strings, these three things are so crucial is because what they chunk up to in the real world, in the mind of your prospect, they say, this is a person worth listening to. You're a person worth listening to. And then even one level above there, because this person, the salesperson can help me achieve my goals, can help me get what I want in life. That's why they listen to you. They don't listen to you so you can get what you put. It's like an early commission or sell you. They listen because they want you to solve a problem and they believe you can. See, we have been conditioned since we were little kids to seek out experts to resolve all problems and remove our pain. Start it back when we were little kids. Our parents took us to the doctor and they were dressed a certain way. They had stethoscope on, white jacket, diplomas on the walls and these doctors were very special people. Even our own parents would defer to the doctor. And when the doctor asked you a question, what did you do? You answered truthfully, forthrightedly, you didn't bob and weed. You didn't say, so tell me how long the problem. How long you had a problem with a doctor? You don't do that, right? You answered the doctor fully and answered why? Because he's got a diploma. He's spent many years studying how to make sick people feel better. He's earned the right to ask you questions. You owe it to him and you should respect him because he's earned that right. You get it. And this is an equation that we all run unconscious. It happens like we see someone, we lay eyes on them. In those first two seconds, we see them, we rip them apart and we put them back together based on how we perceive them. If the perceiver is being sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in their fields, then we say, you know what? I will defer. I'll hand this person control. I'll allow them to ask me questions because they can probably help me resolve my problems. That's what happens. Conversely, if in those first four seconds, they perceive as being dull as dishwater, a disinterested bore, and a stone-pulled fucking novice, good luck trying to close one. They will try to take control. And that was what was happening with my first 12 Stratonites. Essentially, the sale was over before it ever started. They had no shot of closing because they weren't being perceived the right way. That makes sense. I know it does. So the thing is, and I mentioned it for a while, you know, how many seconds? Three seconds, four? Well, many years later, by the time they turned out I was wrong, I said you have four seconds to establish these three crucial things, right? 2015, Harvard and I could get a study, a very detailed study, you can Google it, right? And had to have influence and persuasion and first impressions in business. You know what they found? They found that it's not. It's not four seconds. It's not four seconds to make that first impression. It's five seconds. I was off by a second. I'm sorry. But I could beat them by, what, 20 years almost. But the point is, is that I was off by a second. Maybe I was right there wrong. No, they tested this stuff out and here's what else they found. You want to hear something even more staggering? Not only is the first impression that all important first impression, it happens. They said five seconds, not four. But if you make a negative first impression, it takes you seven subsequent impressions, seven times to change someone's mind about you. I know about you. I don't get seven chances. I have one. When I get into a sales encounter with someone, I know, oops, I fucked up. Let's try the second. Let me try third four. They're not going to give you seven shots. You got one shot to show your stuff. So you must be perceived in exactly the right way. But how? And how do you go about doing that? What is it? I mean, think about it. Is it the words that you say? I mean, what would you say? Bill, I'm sharp as a tack. I'm a mess worker. I swear, I said, what the fuck? The words don't exist. There are no magic words you can say in four seconds to get all that across. Now granted, over the 15, 20, 30 minutes or an hour long sale, yes, you can very much get that point across through words by making your airtight logical case. But in those first few seconds, in that first impression, the words don't exist. The right words, they're just not there. Now, of course, you could say the wrong thing. You say, listen, Bill, I'm a moron. How are you today? Obviously, that's not going to work very well. So there's a very standard thing that we say, but it's not like the words are so special that accomplishes that goal. What is special is, number one, the tonality that we apply to those words. And number two, our body language as we say them, as we deliver them when we're in person. And those two things together, we have tonality and you have body language, make up what's called unconscious communication. I really can't write the letter. Sorry, no, no, no, I don't know what we're doing. Unconscious communication, the words are a conscious communication. Essentially, the words, right, is conscious communication. It's basically analyzed by the conscious mind. Unconscious is our tonality and body language. It slips through the conscious mind, goes through that radar and hits someone directly in the gut and creates an emotion. The way we do that is by using tonality and body language. So we have 10 core tonalities. I'm not going to teach you what to do right now. It's not the purpose of this training. We will go through another intensive and we'll do it next week or a week after, right? And there are certain body language like active listening and other things that we do, how you dress, how you shake hands, how you make eye contact, all these crucial things that all essentially allow you to move forward. So let me go back now. So let's go back now to the straight line. So let's go back. So now what happens is basically this is your straight line. This is your open. This is your close, right? You know, you're basically fucked if you're perceived the wrong way because you don't just tune out and they'll take it wrong. They'll just abuse you and interrupt you, can get your pitch off, right? But remember this, when I say that it's four seconds, what that means is that your unconscious mind, bam, it makes an instant snap decision those first few seconds. And then once that decision gets made, your conscious mind then takes over and now analyzes every word that you're hearing and says, hmm, was my gut reaction correct or incorrect? Glad to see you're doing better My question is in module eight of the presentation you mentioned to never hit the prospect head on about their objection until the very end After multiple loops. Do you still recommend hitting them head on until the end or no? Um I think there's a misunderstanding here. Um, I gotta check that out. It's not it's on the first objection in other words on the first one Hit you with an objection After you ask for the order for the first time you want to deflect that initial objection Right, so you deflect the initial objection All right, and then that allows you to essentially loop back. You know, I hear what you're saying But let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to do you like the idea? That's how you that's how you begin the process of looping, right? And then the loop ends with you asking for the order For a second time, right? That's a loop. It starts with getting an objection Right, so the first time we deflect but if so, let's say someone says let me think about it And you say well, I understand you want to think about it, but let me ask you this Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea again? And you say, yeah, Jordan. I think it's a really good idea. I say exactly to smash it again In fact, let me say this again the true beauty is it's blah blah blah blah, right? And then I take that through all the three tends. I ask for the order and now you say no I Jordan sounds great. Let me just talk to my wife I'm not gonna deflect that now. I have to answer whatever you come back to that when you hop to a new objection I will answer that objection head on so the second time I answer the objection head on But I don't use that answer As a means to go into a close I then loop back again The answer to an objection does not close the sale. It's what you say after You answer the objections. You're a butthole that earns you the right to speak more And here's the thing can you know, so you're I know I see you answer a lot of people's questions and your answers are phenomenal, right? So the rebuttal does matter. There there's there's bad rebuttals There's Average rebuttals and there's great rebuttals. So yeah, the rebuttal and the better the rebuttal the better your chances are of closing But no matter how great a rebuttal is it's not like you could say that you know rebutt someone's objection And it was such a great rebuttal that now you can close them No, it's it's what you say after it's the certainty you build on the backside of that rebuttal That's where the magic truly happens. It's just you That's it, right? So you got to just be really vigilant on the fact that your state just remember that your emotional state is not a Constant it goes up and down throughout the day and if you at any point feel like you're losing that state of certainty You got to fire off your anger get back into a state of certainty Remember like what did I say up there? I said sales is the transference of emotion And the primary emotion you're transferring is certainty now the straight line system doesn't create certainty The straight line system transfers certainty You you're so you have to create the certainty it certainly comes from in your heart and your mind Okay, the straight line shows you how to transfer to another human being but you got to be certain That's where your state comes in if you're in a state of uncertainty Good luck trying to close so focus on your state management as well as everything else in the show State management just so crucial out in the field.
